title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: Lab 2 Demo: Random grid sites
text:
NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab
Open student exercise in Colab

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed.


Goal: Start Lab 2 with a probability question on an equally likely sample space, then show how to compute it by counting and by simulation in R.


Before the 2D grid, we practice the same ideas on a 1D line of 5 sites.
We label sites as:
\[
S = \{1,2,3,4,5\}
\]
We choose 2 distinct sites without replacement, so the equally likely sample space is all 2-site sets:
\[
|S_2| = \binom{5}{2} = 10
\]


The “end sites” are 1 and 5.
Use the complement (“no ends” means both picks are from {2,3,4}):
\[
P(\text{at least one end})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{\binom{5}{2}}
= 1 - \frac{3}{10}
= \frac{7}{10}
\]



Adjacent pairs are:

{1,2}, {2,3}, {3,4}, {4,5} (4 pairs total)

So:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{4}{\binom{5}{2}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4
\]



set.seed(109)

sites <- 1:5
B <- 10000

at_least_one_end <- logical(B)
adjacent <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  draws <- sample(sites, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  a <- draws[1]
  c <- draws[2]

  # "at least one end" using the Lab 1 sum idea
  at_least_one_end[b] <- sum(c(a == 1, a == 5, c == 1, c == 5)) > 0

  # adjacent on a line if they differ by 1
  adjacent[b] <- (abs(a - c) == 1)
}

mean(at_least_one_end)
mean(adjacent)





We have an \(N \times N\) grid of possible sampling sites. Each site is equally likely.
For this demo, we will keep things realistic and simple:

We select 2 distinct sites without replacement from the grid.
Every unordered pair of sites is equally likely.

So the sample space size is:
\[
|S| = \binom{N^2}{2}
\]

Total sites: \(N^2\)
Corner sites: \(4\)
Edge sites (including corners): \(4N - 4\)

Define:

\(p_\text{corner} = \frac{4}{N^2}\)
\(p_\text{edge} = \frac{4N-4}{N^2}\)






Types

numeric vs integer vs character vs logical
class(x) (and optionally typeof(x))

Vectors

c(...) to create vectors
length(x) to check size
bracketing x[i] and bracketing with a logical vector x[x > 5]
element-by-element operations: x + 1, x * 2, x == y

Matrices

matrix(...) to create a matrix
dim(m) to check size
bracketing m[row, col], extracting rows/cols with m[1, ], m[, 2]

Simulation + loops

sample(..., replace = FALSE) (choose distinct sites)
%in% for “is it in this set?”
placeholders: logical(B), numeric(B)
for (b in 1:B) { ... } storing one result per run
probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE)




# Types
x_num <- 3.5
x_int <- 3L
x_chr <- "site"
x_log <- (3 < 5)

class(x_num); class(x_int); class(x_chr); class(x_log)

# Vector + length + scalar operations
v <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
length(v)
v + 1
v * 2

# Matrix + dim + bracketing
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
dim(m)
m[2, 2]
m[1, ]
m[, 2]





We are choosing 2 distinct sites from \(N^2\) equally likely sites.
There are 4 corners and \(N^2 - 4\) non-corners.
Use the complement (“no corners”):
\[
P(\text{at least one corner})
= 1 - P(\text{no corners})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{N^2 - 4}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]




Edges (including corners) total \(4N - 4\) sites, so non-edges total:
\[
N^2 - (4N - 4) = (N-2)^2
\]
Use the complement (“no edges”):
\[
P(\text{at least one edge})
= 1 - P(\text{no edges})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{(N-2)^2}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]




We will say two sites are adjacent if they are 4-neighbors (up/down/left/right).


In an \(N\times N\) grid:

Horizontal adjacent pairs: \(N(N-1)\)
Vertical adjacent pairs: \(N(N-1)\)

So total adjacent (unordered) pairs:
\[
2N(N-1)
\]
Total possible unordered pairs of sites:
\[
\binom{N^2}{2}
\]
Therefore:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{2N(N-1)}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]





Pick values that are easy to talk through in class. For example:

\(N = 5\) (a \(5\times 5\) grid, 25 sites)

Then:

Total pairs: \(\binom{25}{2} = 300\)
Adjacent pairs: \(2N(N-1)=2\cdot 5\cdot 4=40\)

We can compute the three probabilities in R.
N <- 5
total_pairs <- choose(N^2, 2)

# 1) at least one corner
p_at_least_one_corner <- 1 - choose(N^2 - 4, 2) / total_pairs

# 2) at least one edge
p_at_least_one_edge <- 1 - choose((N - 2)^2, 2) / total_pairs

# 3) adjacent
p_adjacent <- (2 * N * (N - 1)) / total_pairs

p_at_least_one_corner
p_at_least_one_edge
p_adjacent




We simulate \(B\) random samples of 2 distinct sites, and estimate probabilities by proportions (mean(TRUE/FALSE)).
We label each grid cell with a string "i,j" so the sampled sites look like coordinates you’d write by hand.
New-to-Lab-1/2 note: this uses paste() to create "i,j" labels and strsplit() + as.numeric() to turn "i,j" back into numbers for the adjacency check.
set.seed(109)

N <- 5
B <- 10000

# Make a grid whose entries look like "i,j" (row,col)
grid <- matrix("", nrow = N, ncol = N)
for (i in 1:N) {
  for (j in 1:N) {
    grid[i, j] <- paste(i, j, sep = ",")
  }
}

corners <- c(grid[1,1], grid[1,N], grid[N,1], grid[N,N])
edges   <- c(grid[1, ], grid[N, ], grid[2:(N-1), 1], grid[2:(N-1), N])

at_least_one_corner <- logical(B)
at_least_one_edge   <- logical(B)
adjacent            <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  picks <- sample(grid, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  s1 <- picks[1]
  s2 <- picks[2]

  rc1 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s1, ",")[[1]])
  rc2 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s2, ",")[[1]])

  r1 <- rc1[1]; c1 <- rc1[2]
  r2 <- rc2[1]; c2 <- rc2[2]

  # keep the Lab 1 "sum of TRUE/FALSE" idea (avoid new | and & operators)
  at_least_one_corner[b] <- sum(c(s1 %in% corners, s2 %in% corners)) > 0
  at_least_one_edge[b]   <- sum(c(s1 %in% edges,   s2 %in% edges))   > 0

  same_row_adj <- (r1 == r2) * (abs(c1 - c2) == 1)
  same_col_adj <- (c1 == c2) * (abs(r1 - r2) == 1)
  adjacent[b] <- sum(c(same_row_adj, same_col_adj)) > 0
}

mean(at_least_one_corner)
mean(at_least_one_edge)
mean(adjacent)





Equally likely sample space: every 2-site sample (without replacement) is equally likely.
Counting: corners = 4; edges = \(4N-4\); total sites = \(N^2\); total pairs = \(\binom{N^2}{2}\).
Matrices + bracketing: grid <- matrix("", ...), corners <- c(grid[1,1], ...), etc.
For loop: pre-allocate storage (logical(B)) and store one result per iteration.
Probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE) gives a proportion.

title: Lab 10
text:
Learning outcomes
By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

Count and locate missing values (is.na(), summaries per column).
Choose a strategy: drop incomplete rows for an analysis, or report how many were removed.
Load a CSV from the web with read_csv() using a raw GitHub URL (TidyTuesday).
Load a CSV you uploaded in Google Colab using the file path (often under /content/).
For one numerical and one categorical variable: compute group summaries and make boxplots (and related plots).
For two numerical variables: make a scatterplot and describe the pattern in words.
For two categorical variables: build a two-way table of counts, row/column/total proportions, and stacked bar charts.

title: Lab 10
text:
Summary of new functions







Function / pattern
What it does




read_csv("url_or_path")
Reads a CSV from the web or from a file path (e.g. after upload in Colab).


is.na(x), sum(is.na(x))
Detects missing values; counts how many in a vector.


summarize(across(everything(), ~ …))
Applies a summary (e.g. count of NA) to every column.


drop_na(col1, col2, …)
Drops rows with missing values in any of the listed columns.


count(A, B)
Counts rows for each combination of two variables (long format).


pivot_wider()
Turns long counts into a two-way table with column names from one variable.


group_by() + mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
Converts cell counts to proportions within each group (row or column, depending on what you group by).


group_by(cat) + summarize()
Numeric summaries of one variable within each category.


ggplot() + geom_boxplot()
Compares a numerical variable across categories.


ggplot() + geom_point()
Scatterplot for two numerical variables.


ggplot() + geom_col()
Stacked bar chart when you map fill to a second categorical variable.


geom_col(position = "fill")
100% stacked bars (proportions within each bar); often with scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent()).

Part 1, Read a TidyTuesday CSV from the web
Many TidyTuesday datasets are stored on GitHub. Open the dataset’s folder in the TidyTuesday repo, open the CSV, click Raw, and copy the URL from the address bar. Pass that string to read_csv().
We use the Palmer Penguins file published for TidyTuesday (same data idea as the palmerpenguins package, but loaded by URL so you practice the workflow you will use on other weeks).

penguins <- read_csv(

Part 2, Identify and handle missing values

2A) How many missing values per column?


Code
penguins |>
  summarize(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n_missing") |>
  filter(n_missing > 0)


# A tibble: 5 × 2

  <chr>                 <int>
1 bill_length_mm            2
2 bill_depth_mm             2
3 flipper_length_mm         2
4 body_mass_g               2

2B) Drop rows missing key variables for an analysis
For example, to compare species and island and use bill measurements, keep rows that have all of these non-missing:


Code
pg_clean <- penguins |>
  drop_na(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)

nrow(penguins)


[1] 344


Code
nrow(pg_clean)


[1] 342


You can instead filter(!is.na(...)) for the same columns. For Project 3, say in your report whether you dropped rows and how many.

Part 4, Bivariate summaries and plots (all pair types)
Use pg_clean from Part 2 so plots are not full of NA warnings.


Code
pg_clean <- penguins |>
  drop_na(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, body_mass_g)



4A) Lecture 25, One numerical and one categorical variable
Summaries by group (species is categorical; bill_length_mm is numerical):


Code
pg_clean |>
  group_by(species) |>

    n = n(), mean_bill_mm = mean(bill_length_mm), sd_bill_mm = sd(bill_length_mm), median_bill_mm = median(bill_length_mm)

Checklist toward Project 3

title: Lab 11
text:
NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab — virtual population simulation walk-through
Open student exercise in Colab

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed. Save a copy to your Drive before editing the student notebook.




In this lab you will build a small virtual world (sometimes called a digital twin of a study): you write down what is true in the entire population, for example a mean \(\mu\), a standard deviation \(\sigma\), or success probabilities \(p\), \(p_1\), and \(p_2\)—and then you use to draw a random sample from that world. After that, you pretend you are a resident of the world who only sees the sample. You run summaries and tests (mean(), t.test(), prop.test(), …) exactly as you would on real data.
The point of knowing the “ground truth” population is that you get two checks you almost never get with real data:

Statistical method. Did you pick a tool that matches the variable types and the question (for example a two-sample t-test for independent numerical groups, or prop.test for two proportions)? When you already know whether \(H_0\) should hold (e.g. you built the world with \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\) or with \(p_1 = p_2\)), you can see whether the conclusion from the sample is usually consistent with that truth. Sometimes a small p-value appears anyway: remember that Type I error? If we use a significance level of 0.05, then 5% of our samples will lead us to incorrectly reject the null hypothesis.
Correctness of code. Bugs, wrong column names, reversed groups, or a mis-specified prop.test call can all produce output that looks fine but is actually incorrect . In a virtual world you can compare what the code inferred from the sample to what you coded as true for the population. If they disagree in a systematic way, the problem is likely your pipeline (or code suggested by a chat tool).

So the virtual world is a controlled place to validate both your choice of method and your implementation in code.
Throughout this lab:

Population quantities use Greek letters or symbols as in lecture (e.g. \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(p\), \(p_1\), \(p_2\)).
Sample quantities are what you compute from the simulated rows (e.g. \(\hat{p}=x/n\) as the proportion of successes in n trials in the dataset, \(\bar{x}\) as the sample mean of a numerical variable from \(n\) items or people).



Code
library(tidyverse)






For each person \(i\), let \(Y_i = 1\) if a condition is present (“success”) and \(Y_i = 0\) otherwise. In the population, \(p = P(Y_i = 1)\) is the proportion (same as the mean of the 0/1 outcomes).
We simulate \(n\) independent people with rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p): each draw is one Bernoulli trial with success probability p (the argument prob in R is that population probability).


Code
set.seed(1101)

# Population (universe) you choose:
p <- 0.22 # P(Y = 1) for a randomly chosen person
n <- 400

y <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p)

# Sample proportion (same as mean of 0/1):
p_hat <- mean(y)

tibble(
  population_parameter = p,
  sample_size = n,
  sample_proportion_p_hat = p_hat
)


# A tibble: 1 × 3
  population_parameter sample_size sample_proportion_p_hat
                 <dbl>       <dbl>                   <dbl>
1                 0.22         400                   0.198


Read the output like a resident of the universe. You set \(p = 0.22\). The simulated \(\hat{p}\) will be close to \(0.22\) when \(n\) is large, but not exactly equal, because the sample is random.
Try it: Change n to 40 and re-run. How far can \(\hat{p}\) drift from \(p\) when the sample is small?




Continuous measurements (scores, heights, reaction times) are sometimes modeled with a Normal universe in these labs: each observation is rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma).

\(\mu\) is the population mean (the mean argument in rnorm).
\(\sigma\) is the population standard deviation (the sd argument in rnorm).
The sample mean \(\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i\) is computed with mean(y) in R. It only estimates \(\mu\); it is not the same symbol as \(\mu\).



Code
set.seed(1102)

mu <- 100 # population mean score
sigma <- 15 # population SD
n <- 200

y <- rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma)
y_bar <- mean(y)

tibble(
  population_mean_mu = mu,
  population_sd_sigma = sigma,
  sample_size = n,
  sample_mean_y_bar = y_bar
)


# A tibble: 1 × 4
  population_mean_mu population_sd_sigma sample_size sample_mean_y_bar
               <dbl>               <dbl>       <dbl>             <dbl>
1                100                  15         200              97.0


Note: rbinom is for counts out of a fixed number of trials or for binary 0/1 data. It is not used for an arbitrary continuous numerical outcome; that role belongs to rnorm (or other models later in statistics).




You create a data frame with a group column and a numerical y. Group 1 has \(y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma)\) and group 2 has \(y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma)\). You can use the same \(\sigma\) in both groups for simplicity (a teaching choice, not a rule about the real world).




Code
set.seed(1103)

mu1 <- 50
mu2 <- 50 # same population mean in both groups
sigma <- 12
n_per <- 60

d_num_null <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu1, sd = sigma),
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu2, sd = sigma)
  )
)

d_num_null |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(sample_mean = mean(y), sample_sd = sd(y), n = n(), .groups = "drop")


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  group sample_mean sample_sd     n
  <fct>       <dbl>     <dbl> <int>
1 A            53.1      10.3    60
2 B            49.6      13.5    60


Hypotheses for Welch two-sample t-test (what t.test(y ~ group, var.equal = FALSE) uses by default):

\(H_0\): \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\) (the population mean of \(y\) is the same in group A and group B).
\(H_a\): \(\mu_1 \neq \mu_2\) (two-sided by default).

Here \(\mu_1\) is the mean of \(y\) among all units that would be labeled A in the population, and \(\mu_2\) is the same for B.


Code
t.test(y ~ group, data = d_num_null, var.equal = FALSE)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  y by group
t = 1.573, df = 110.12, p-value = 0.1186
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group A and group B is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.8971371  7.8025917
sample estimates:
mean in group A mean in group B 
       53.10232        49.64959 


Interpretation template. The p-value summarizes how compatible these sample group means are with “no difference in population means” (\(H_0\)) when random sampling is the only source of discrepancy. Because you built the world with \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\)`, a large p-value is common; a small p-value can still happen by chance (Type I error idea, Lecture 17).





Code
set.seed(1104)

mu1 <- 50
mu2 <- 58 # different population means
sigma <- 12
n_per <- 60

d_num_alt <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu1, sd = sigma),
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu2, sd = sigma)
  )
)

t.test(y ~ group, data = d_num_alt, var.equal = FALSE)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  y by group
t = -6.1973, df = 116.53, p-value = 8.951e-09
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group A and group B is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -18.836046  -9.712518
sample estimates:
mean in group A mean in group B 
       46.80788        61.08216 


Try it: Re-run 3A and 3B a few times with the same seeds removed (delete set.seed lines or change seeds). Watch the p-value move even when \(H_0\) is true in 3A.





For a binary outcome coded 0/1, \(p_1\) is the population proportion of successes in group 1 and \(p_2\) in group 2.




Code
set.seed(1105)

p1 <- 0.18
p2 <- 0.18 # same population success probability
n_per <- 120

d_bin_null <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p1),
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p2)
  )
)

counts_null <- d_bin_null |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(successes = sum(y == 1), n = n(), p_hat = mean(y), .groups = "drop")

counts_null


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  group successes     n p_hat
  <fct>     <int> <int> <dbl>
1 A            21   120 0.175
2 B            18   120 0.15 


Hypotheses for prop.test comparing two proportions:

\(H_0\): \(p_1 = p_2\).
\(H_a\): \(p_1 \neq p_2\) (two-sided default).



Code
prop.test(x = counts_null$successes, n = counts_null$n, correct = FALSE)



    2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction

data:  counts_null$successes out of counts_null$n
X-squared = 0.27555, df = 1, p-value = 0.5996
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.06829151  0.11829151
sample estimates:
prop 1 prop 2 
 0.175  0.150 


The output includes a 95% confidence interval for \(p_1 - p_2\) (difference in population proportions) and a p-value for the same \(H_0\).





Code
set.seed(1106)

p1 <- 0.18
p2 <- 0.32
n_per <- 120

d_bin_alt <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p1),
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p2)
  )
)

counts_alt <- d_bin_alt |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(successes = sum(y == 1), n = n(), p_hat = mean(y), .groups = "drop")

prop.test(x = counts_alt$successes, n = counts_alt$n, correct = FALSE)



    2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction

data:  counts_alt$successes out of counts_alt$n
X-squared = 7.6167, df = 1, p-value = 0.005783
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.26897883 -0.04768783
sample estimates:
   prop 1    prop 2 
0.1916667 0.3500000 


Check: The sample proportions \(\hat{p}_1\) and \(\hat{p}_2\) should be near p1 and p2, and the p-value for \(H_0: p_1 = p_2\) is often small when \(p_1 \neq p_2\) and \(n\) is reasonably large.













Idea
Population (universe)
Sample (what you compute in R)




Binary one group
\(p = P(Y=1)\)
\(\hat{p} = \texttt{mean(y)}\) for 0/1 data


Numerical one group
\(\mu\), \(\sigma\)
\(\bar{y} = \texttt{mean(y)}\), \(s = \texttt{sd(y)}\)


Two numerical groups
\(\mu_1\), \(\mu_2\)
group means from summarise(mean = mean(y))


Two binary groups
\(p_1\), \(p_2\)
\(\hat{p}_1\), \(\hat{p}_2\) from group means of 0/1



Tests ask about population parameters, not sample quantities. For example, the Welch t-test uses \(\bar{y}_1\) and \(\bar{y}_2\) to weigh evidence about whether \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\) is plausibly 0 after accounting for sampling variability.






The city piloted “GreenSteps” walking groups (A) versus usual activity (B). Enrollment was not randomized: staff recruited more heavily in neighborhoods that already had higher walkability scores (a categorical summary: walk = "easy" vs "hard"). In our simulation, being assigned to a walking group does not change anyone’s weekly steps — only walkability changes steps. Still, a naive comparison of steps by group can look like GreenSteps “works,” because walkability is a confounder: it is associated with both group and steps.





Code
set.seed(1107)

n <- 600

d_conf <- tibble(
  walk = factor(
    sample(c("hard", "easy"), size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.55, 0.45)),
    levels = c("hard", "easy")
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    # Group assignment depends on walkability (observational recruitment)
    p_green = if_else(walk == "easy", 0.62, 0.22),
    group = factor(
      ifelse(rbinom(n(), 1, prob = p_green) == 1, "GreenSteps", "Usual"),
      levels = c("Usual", "GreenSteps")
    ),
    # Weekly steps: depend ONLY on walkability (+ noise). No arrow from group.
    mu_steps = if_else(walk == "easy", 9800, 7200),
    steps = rnorm(n(), mean = mu_steps, sd = 900)
  )


Crude comparison (ignores walkability):


Code
t.test(steps ~ group, data = d_conf, var.equal = FALSE)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  steps by group
t = -8.1385, df = 511.28, p-value = 3.068e-15
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Usual and group GreenSteps is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -1304.0433  -796.8844
sample estimates:
     mean in group Usual mean in group GreenSteps 
                7956.358                 9006.821 


Stratified description (hold walkability fixed — subclassification):


Code
d_conf |>
  group_by(walk, group) |>
  summarise(mean_steps = mean(steps), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(walk, group)


# A tibble: 4 × 4
  walk  group      mean_steps     n
  <fct> <fct>           <dbl> <int>
1 hard  Usual           7146.   252
2 hard  GreenSteps      7129.    75
3 easy  Usual           9883.   106
4 easy  GreenSteps      9850.   167


What you should see. The crude t-test often suggests a difference in population mean steps between GreenSteps and Usual. Within each level of walk, however, mean steps for GreenSteps and Usual should be similar (any small differences are just Monte Carlo noise), matching the fact that in this universe group does not change steps — walkability drives both recruitment and steps.
Conclusion in words. This is a toy virtual world where a resident who only runs t.test(steps ~ group) can be misled about causation. Adding the third variable and comparing within walk strata shows the structure you imposed: confounding, not a treatment effect.

title: Lab 11
text:
In this lab you will build a small virtual world (sometimes called a digital twin of a study): you write down what is true in the entire population, for example a mean \(\mu\), a standard deviation \(\sigma\), or success probabilities \(p\), \(p_1\), and \(p_2\)—and then you use to draw a random sample from that world. After that, you pretend you are a resident of the world who only sees the sample. You run summaries and tests (mean(), t.test(), prop.test(), …) exactly as you would on real data.
The point of knowing the “ground truth” population is that you get two checks you almost never get with real data:

Statistical method. Did you pick a tool that matches the variable types and the question (for example a two-sample t-test for independent numerical groups, or prop.test for two proportions)? When you already know whether \(H_0\) should hold (e.g. you built the world with \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\) or with \(p_1 = p_2\)), you can see whether the conclusion from the sample is usually consistent with that truth. Sometimes a small p-value appears anyway: remember that Type I error? If we use a significance level of 0.05, then 5% of our samples will lead us to incorrectly reject the null hypothesis.
Correctness of code. Bugs, wrong column names, reversed groups, or a mis-specified prop.test call can all produce output that looks fine but is actually incorrect . In a virtual world you can compare what the code inferred from the sample to what you coded as true for the population. If they disagree in a systematic way, the problem is likely your pipeline (or code suggested by a chat tool).

So the virtual world is a controlled place to validate both your choice of method and your implementation in code.
Throughout this lab:

Population quantities use Greek letters or symbols as in lecture (e.g. \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(p\), \(p_1\), \(p_2\)).
Sample quantities are what you compute from the simulated rows (e.g. \(\hat{p}=x/n\) as the proportion of successes in n trials in the dataset, \(\bar{x}\) as the sample mean of a numerical variable from \(n\) items or people).



Code
library(tidyverse)

title: Lab 11
text:
For each person \(i\), let \(Y_i = 1\) if a condition is present (“success”) and \(Y_i = 0\) otherwise. In the population, \(p = P(Y_i = 1)\) is the proportion (same as the mean of the 0/1 outcomes).
We simulate \(n\) independent people with rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p): each draw is one Bernoulli trial with success probability p (the argument prob in R is that population probability).


Code
set.seed(1101)

# Population (universe) you choose:
p <- 0.22 # P(Y = 1) for a randomly chosen person
n <- 400

y <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p)

# Sample proportion (same as mean of 0/1):
p_hat <- mean(y)

tibble(
  population_parameter = p,
  sample_size = n,
  sample_proportion_p_hat = p_hat
)


# A tibble: 1 × 3
  population_parameter sample_size sample_proportion_p_hat
                 <dbl>       <dbl>                   <dbl>
1                 0.22         400                   0.198


Read the output like a resident of the universe. You set \(p = 0.22\). The simulated \(\hat{p}\) will be close to \(0.22\) when \(n\) is large, but not exactly equal, because the sample is random.
Try it: Change n to 40 and re-run. How far can \(\hat{p}\) drift from \(p\) when the sample is small?

title: Lab 11
text:
Continuous measurements (scores, heights, reaction times) are sometimes modeled with a Normal universe in these labs: each observation is rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma).

\(\mu\) is the population mean (the mean argument in rnorm).
\(\sigma\) is the population standard deviation (the sd argument in rnorm).
The sample mean \(\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i\) is computed with mean(y) in R. It only estimates \(\mu\); it is not the same symbol as \(\mu\).



Code
set.seed(1102)

mu <- 100 # population mean score
sigma <- 15 # population SD
n <- 200

y <- rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma)
y_bar <- mean(y)

tibble(
  population_mean_mu = mu,
  population_sd_sigma = sigma,
  sample_size = n,
  sample_mean_y_bar = y_bar
)


# A tibble: 1 × 4
  population_mean_mu population_sd_sigma sample_size sample_mean_y_bar
               <dbl>               <dbl>       <dbl>             <dbl>
1                100                  15         200              97.0


Note: rbinom is for counts out of a fixed number of trials or for binary 0/1 data. It is not used for an arbitrary continuous numerical outcome; that role belongs to rnorm (or other models later in statistics).

title: Lab 11
text:
You create a data frame with a group column and a numerical y. Group 1 has \(y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma)\) and group 2 has \(y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma)\). You can use the same \(\sigma\) in both groups for simplicity (a teaching choice, not a rule about the real world).




Code
set.seed(1103)

mu1 <- 50
mu2 <- 50 # same population mean in both groups
sigma <- 12
n_per <- 60

d_num_null <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu1, sd = sigma),
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu2, sd = sigma)
  )
)

d_num_null |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(sample_mean = mean(y), sample_sd = sd(y), n = n(), .groups = "drop")


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  group sample_mean sample_sd     n
  <fct>       <dbl>     <dbl> <int>
1 A            53.1      10.3    60
2 B            49.6      13.5    60


Hypotheses for Welch two-sample t-test (what t.test(y ~ group, var.equal = FALSE) uses by default):

\(H_0\): \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\) (the population mean of \(y\) is the same in group A and group B).
\(H_a\): \(\mu_1 \neq \mu_2\) (two-sided by default).

Here \(\mu_1\) is the mean of \(y\) among all units that would be labeled A in the population, and \(\mu_2\) is the same for B.


Code
t.test(y ~ group, data = d_num_null, var.equal = FALSE)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  y by group
t = 1.573, df = 110.12, p-value = 0.1186
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group A and group B is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.8971371  7.8025917
sample estimates:
mean in group A mean in group B 
       53.10232        49.64959 


Interpretation template. The p-value summarizes how compatible these sample group means are with “no difference in population means” (\(H_0\)) when random sampling is the only source of discrepancy. Because you built the world with \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\)`, a large p-value is common; a small p-value can still happen by chance (Type I error idea, Lecture 17).





Code
set.seed(1104)

mu1 <- 50
mu2 <- 58 # different population means
sigma <- 12
n_per <- 60

d_num_alt <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu1, sd = sigma),
    rnorm(n_per, mean = mu2, sd = sigma)
  )
)

t.test(y ~ group, data = d_num_alt, var.equal = FALSE)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  y by group
t = -6.1973, df = 116.53, p-value = 8.951e-09
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group A and group B is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -18.836046  -9.712518
sample estimates:
mean in group A mean in group B 
       46.80788        61.08216 


Try it: Re-run 3A and 3B a few times with the same seeds removed (delete set.seed lines or change seeds). Watch the p-value move even when \(H_0\) is true in 3A.

title: Lab 11
text:
For a binary outcome coded 0/1, \(p_1\) is the population proportion of successes in group 1 and \(p_2\) in group 2.




Code
set.seed(1105)

p1 <- 0.18
p2 <- 0.18 # same population success probability
n_per <- 120

d_bin_null <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p1),
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p2)
  )
)

counts_null <- d_bin_null |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(successes = sum(y == 1), n = n(), p_hat = mean(y), .groups = "drop")

counts_null


# A tibble: 2 × 4
  group successes     n p_hat
  <fct>     <int> <int> <dbl>
1 A            21   120 0.175
2 B            18   120 0.15 


Hypotheses for prop.test comparing two proportions:

\(H_0\): \(p_1 = p_2\).
\(H_a\): \(p_1 \neq p_2\) (two-sided default).



Code
prop.test(x = counts_null$successes, n = counts_null$n, correct = FALSE)



    2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction

data:  counts_null$successes out of counts_null$n
X-squared = 0.27555, df = 1, p-value = 0.5996
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.06829151  0.11829151
sample estimates:
prop 1 prop 2 
 0.175  0.150 


The output includes a 95% confidence interval for \(p_1 - p_2\) (difference in population proportions) and a p-value for the same \(H_0\).





Code
set.seed(1106)

p1 <- 0.18
p2 <- 0.32
n_per <- 120

d_bin_alt <- tibble(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_per), levels = c("A", "B")),
  y = c(
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p1),
    rbinom(n_per, size = 1, prob = p2)
  )
)

counts_alt <- d_bin_alt |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(successes = sum(y == 1), n = n(), p_hat = mean(y), .groups = "drop")

prop.test(x = counts_alt$successes, n = counts_alt$n, correct = FALSE)



    2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction

data:  counts_alt$successes out of counts_alt$n
X-squared = 7.6167, df = 1, p-value = 0.005783
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.26897883 -0.04768783
sample estimates:
   prop 1    prop 2 
0.1916667 0.3500000 


Check: The sample proportions \(\hat{p}_1\) and \(\hat{p}_2\) should be near p1 and p2, and the p-value for \(H_0: p_1 = p_2\) is often small when \(p_1 \neq p_2\) and \(n\) is reasonably large.

title: Lab 11
text:
Idea
Population (universe)
Sample (what you compute in R)




Binary one group
\(p = P(Y=1)\)
\(\hat{p} = \texttt{mean(y)}\) for 0/1 data


Numerical one group
\(\mu\), \(\sigma\)
\(\bar{y} = \texttt{mean(y)}\), \(s = \texttt{sd(y)}\)


Two numerical groups
\(\mu_1\), \(\mu_2\)
group means from summarise(mean = mean(y))


Two binary groups
\(p_1\), \(p_2\)
\(\hat{p}_1\), \(\hat{p}_2\) from group means of 0/1



Tests ask about population parameters, not sample quantities. For example, the Welch t-test uses \(\bar{y}_1\) and \(\bar{y}_2\) to weigh evidence about whether \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\) is plausibly 0 after accounting for sampling variability.

title: Lab 11
text:
The city piloted “GreenSteps” walking groups (A) versus usual activity (B). Enrollment was not randomized: staff recruited more heavily in neighborhoods that already had higher walkability scores (a categorical summary: walk = "easy" vs "hard"). In our simulation, being assigned to a walking group does not change anyone’s weekly steps — only walkability changes steps. Still, a naive comparison of steps by group can look like GreenSteps “works,” because walkability is a confounder: it is associated with both group and steps.





Code
set.seed(1107)

n <- 600

d_conf <- tibble(
  walk = factor(
    sample(c("hard", "easy"), size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.55, 0.45)),
    levels = c("hard", "easy")
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    # Group assignment depends on walkability (observational recruitment)
    p_green = if_else(walk == "easy", 0.62, 0.22),
    group = factor(
      ifelse(rbinom(n(), 1, prob = p_green) == 1, "GreenSteps", "Usual"),
      levels = c("Usual", "GreenSteps")
    ),
    # Weekly steps: depend ONLY on walkability (+ noise). No arrow from group.
    mu_steps = if_else(walk == "easy", 9800, 7200),
    steps = rnorm(n(), mean = mu_steps, sd = 900)
  )


Crude comparison (ignores walkability):


Code
t.test(steps ~ group, data = d_conf, var.equal = FALSE)



    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  steps by group
t = -8.1385, df = 511.28, p-value = 3.068e-15
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Usual and group GreenSteps is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -1304.0433  -796.8844
sample estimates:
     mean in group Usual mean in group GreenSteps 
                7956.358                 9006.821 


Stratified description (hold walkability fixed — subclassification):


Code
d_conf |>
  group_by(walk, group) |>
  summarise(mean_steps = mean(steps), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(walk, group)


# A tibble: 4 × 4
  walk  group      mean_steps     n
  <fct> <fct>           <dbl> <int>
1 hard  Usual           7146.   252
2 hard  GreenSteps      7129.    75
3 easy  Usual           9883.   106
4 easy  GreenSteps      9850.   167


What you should see. The crude t-test often suggests a difference in population mean steps between GreenSteps and Usual. Within each level of walk, however, mean steps for GreenSteps and Usual should be similar (any small differences are just Monte Carlo noise), matching the fact that in this universe group does not change steps — walkability drives both recruitment and steps.
Conclusion in words. This is a toy virtual world where a resident who only runs t.test(steps ~ group) can be misled about causation. Adding the third variable and comparing within walk strata shows the structure you imposed: confounding, not a treatment effect.

title: Lab 12
text:
NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab — linear and logistic regression examples
Open student exercise in Colab — trees practice template

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed. Save a copy to your Drive before editing the student notebook.





By the end of this demo lab, you should be able to:

Describe linear association with scatterplots (form, direction, strength) and cor().
Explain how correlation changes for weak/moderate/strong positive and negative relationships.
Identify when r near 0 reflects weak/no linear association.
Recognize nonlinear patterns where linear methods should not be used.
Fit simple linear regression with lm(), make predictions, and compute confidence/prediction intervals with predict().
Fit simple logistic regression with glm(..., family = binomial), predict probabilities, and use a cutoff for classification.







Code
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}
library(tidyverse)










Code
set.seed(1212)
n <- 120
x <- runif(n, 0, 10)

d_pos_strong <- tibble(example = "positive_strong", x = x, y = 2 + 1.9 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 1.0))
d_pos_mod    <- tibble(example = "positive_moderate", x = x, y = 2 + 1.8 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 3.0))
d_pos_weak   <- tibble(example = "positive_weak", x = x, y = 2 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 6.5))

d_neg_strong <- tibble(example = "negative_strong", x = x, y = 24 - 1.8 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 1.0))
d_neg_mod    <- tibble(example = "negative_moderate", x = x, y = 24 - 1.6 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 3.0))
d_neg_weak   <- tibble(example = "negative_weak", x = x, y = 24 - 1.3 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 6.5))

d_none       <- tibble(example = "no_linear_relationship", x = x, y = rnorm(n, 12, 4))
d_nonlinear  <- tibble(example = "nonlinear_curve", x = x, y = 0.7 * (x - 5)^2 + rnorm(n, 0, 1.2))

demo_cor <- bind_rows(
  d_pos_strong, d_pos_mod, d_pos_weak,
  d_neg_strong, d_neg_mod, d_neg_weak,
  d_none, d_nonlinear
)







Code
cor_table <- demo_cor |>
  group_by(example) |>
  summarise(r = cor(x, y), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(r))

cor_table


# A tibble: 8 × 2
  example                     r
  <chr>                   <dbl>
1 positive_strong         0.983
2 positive_moderate       0.891
3 positive_weak           0.548
4 no_linear_relationship -0.108
5 nonlinear_curve        -0.191
6 negative_weak          -0.396
7 negative_moderate      -0.832
8 negative_strong        -0.978







Code
ggplot(demo_cor, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75, color = "darkgreen") +
  facet_wrap(~ example, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship type demo (bio/nature themed synthetic data)",
    x = "Explanatory variable (x)",
    y = "Response variable (y)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)









Interpretation reminders:

Closer to 1 or -1 means stronger linear relationship.
Closer to 0 means weak to no linear relationship.
Sign gives direction (+ increasing, - decreasing).
For nonlinear_curve, do not apply linear-correlation/regression conclusions as if form were linear.






Example: predict plant height from sunlight hours.


Code
set.seed(1213)
plant <- tibble(
  sunlight_hours = runif(140, 2, 12),
  height_cm = 9 + 2.7 * sunlight_hours + rnorm(140, 0, 4)
)

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(plant)), size = 0.8 * nrow(plant))
train <- plant[idx, ]
test  <- plant[-idx, ]






Code
fit_lin <- lm(height_cm ~ sunlight_hours, data = train)
summary(fit_lin)



Call:
lm(formula = height_cm ~ sunlight_hours, data = train)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-10.866  -2.733   0.135   2.919   8.536 

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)      9.4801     0.9544   9.934   <2e-16 ***
sunlight_hours   2.6719     0.1268  21.075   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 3.836 on 110 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8015,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7997 
F-statistic: 444.2 on 1 and 110 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


Code
coef(fit_lin)


   (Intercept) sunlight_hours 
      9.480129       2.671868 







Code
new_x <- tibble(sunlight_hours = 8)

predict(fit_lin, newdata = new_x)  # point prediction


       1 
30.85507 


Code
predict(fit_lin, newdata = new_x, interval = "confidence")  # mean response CI


       fit      lwr      upr
1 30.85507 30.09108 31.61907


Code
predict(fit_lin, newdata = new_x, interval = "prediction")  # individual PI


       fit      lwr      upr
1 30.85507 23.21522 38.49493







Code
train <- train |>
  mutate(pred = predict(fit_lin, newdata = train),
         resid = height_cm - pred)

test <- test |>
  mutate(pred = predict(fit_lin, newdata = test))

mse <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

tibble(
  train_MSE = mse(train$height_cm, train$pred),
  test_MSE = mse(test$height_cm, test$pred),
  test_R2 = 1 - sum((test$height_cm - test$pred)^2) /
    sum((test$height_cm - mean(test$height_cm))^2)
)

Example: model probability that a seed germinates from soil moisture.


Code
set.seed(1214)
seed <- tibble(
  moisture = runif(180, 5, 45)
) |>
  mutate(
    p_germ = plogis(-3.2 + 0.14 * moisture),
    germinated = rbinom(n(), 1, p_germ)
  )

idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(seed)), size = 0.8 * nrow(seed))
seed_train <- seed[idx, ]
seed_test  <- seed[-idx, ]






Code

summary(fit_log)

seed_test <- seed_test |>
  mutate(
    p_hat = predict(fit_log, newdata = seed_test, type = "response"),
    y_hat = if_else(p_hat >= 0.5, 1, 0)
  )

tibble(
  test_accuracy = mean(seed_test$germinated == seed_test$y_hat),
  p_at_20 = predict(fit_log, newdata = tibble(moisture = 20), type = "response"),

Use cor() and scatterplots to summarize linear association only.
Use lm() + predict() for linear prediction and intervals.
Use glm(..., family = binomial) for binary outcomes and probability prediction.
Always check whether linear form is reasonable before applying linear methods.

  facet_wrap(~ example, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship type demo (bio/nature themed synthetic data)",
    x = "Explanatory variable (x)",
    y = "Response variable (y)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)









Interpretation reminders:

Closer to 1 or -1 means stronger linear relationship.
Closer to 0 means weak to no linear relationship.
Sign gives direction (+ increasing, - decreasing).
For nonlinear_curve, do not apply linear-correlation/regression conclusions as if form were linear.

title: Lab 2
text:
Demo page source: Lab 2 Demo (published page)






NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab
Open student exercise in Colab

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed. Save a copy to your Drive (File → Save a copy in Drive) before editing the student notebook.



Goal: Start Lab 2 with a probability question on an equally likely sample space, then show how to compute it by counting and by simulation in R.


Before the 2D grid, we practice the same ideas on a 1D line of 5 sites.
We label sites as:
\[
S = \{1,2,3,4,5\}
\]
We choose 2 distinct sites without replacement, so the equally likely sample space is all 2-site sets:
\[
|S_2| = \binom{5}{2} = 10
\]


The “end sites” are 1 and 5.
Use the complement (“no ends” means both picks are from {2,3,4}):
\[
P(\text{at least one end})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{\binom{5}{2}}
= 1 - \frac{3}{10}
= \frac{7}{10}
\]



Adjacent pairs are:

{1,2}, {2,3}, {3,4}, {4,5} (4 pairs total)

So:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{4}{\binom{5}{2}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4
\]





Code
set.seed(109)

sites <- 1:5
B <- 10000

at_least_one_end <- logical(B)
adjacent <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  draws <- sample(sites, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  a <- draws[1]
  c <- draws[2]

  # "at least one end" using the Lab 1 sum idea
  at_least_one_end[b] <- sum(c(a == 1, a == 5, c == 1, c == 5)) > 0

  # adjacent on a line if they differ by 1
  adjacent[b] <- (abs(a - c) == 1)
}

mean(at_least_one_end)


[1] 0.7015


Code
mean(adjacent)


[1] 0.4008







We have an (N N) grid of possible sampling sites. Each site is equally likely.
For this demo, we will keep things realistic and simple:

We select 2 distinct sites without replacement from the grid.
Every unordered pair of sites is equally likely.

So the sample space size is:
\[
|S| = \binom{N^2}{2}
\]

Total sites: (N^2)
Corner sites: (4)
Edge sites (including corners): (4N - 4)

Define:

(p_ = )
(p_ = )






Types

numeric vs integer vs character vs logical
class(x) (and optionally typeof(x))

Vectors

c(...) to create vectors
length(x) to check size
bracketing x[i] and bracketing with a logical vector x[x > 5]
element-by-element operations: x + 1, x * 2, x == y

Matrices

matrix(...) to create a matrix
dim(m) to check size
bracketing m[row, col], extracting rows/cols with m[1, ], m[, 2]

Simulation + loops

sample(..., replace = FALSE) (choose distinct sites)
%in% for “is it in this set?”
placeholders: logical(B), numeric(B)
for (b in 1:B) { ... } storing one result per run
probability by simulation: mean(TRUE/FALSE)






Code
# Types
x_num <- 3.5
x_int <- 3L
x_chr <- "site"
x_log <- (3 < 5)

class(x_num); class(x_int); class(x_chr); class(x_log)


[1] "numeric"


[1] "integer"


[1] "character"


[1] "logical"


Code
# Vector + length + scalar operations
v <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
length(v)


[1] 5


Code
v + 1


[1]  3  5  7  9 11


Code
v * 2


[1]  4  8 12 16 20


Code
# Matrix + dim + bracketing
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)
dim(m)


[1] 2 3


Code
m[2, 2]


[1] 4


Code
m[1, ]


[1] 1 3 5


Code
m[, 2]


[1] 3 4







We are choosing 2 distinct sites from (N^2) equally likely sites.
There are 4 corners and (N^2 - 4) non-corners.
Use the complement (“no corners”):
\[
P(\text{at least one corner})
= 1 - P(\text{no corners})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{N^2 - 4}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]




Edges (including corners) total (4N - 4) sites, so non-edges total:
\[
N^2 - (4N - 4) = (N-2)^2
\]
Use the complement (“no edges”):
\[
P(\text{at least one edge})
= 1 - P(\text{no edges})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{(N-2)^2}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]




We will say two sites are adjacent if they are 4-neighbors (up/down/left/right).


In an (NN) grid:

Horizontal adjacent pairs: (N(N-1))
Vertical adjacent pairs: (N(N-1))

So total adjacent (unordered) pairs:
\[
2N(N-1)
\]
Total possible unordered pairs of sites:
\[
\binom{N^2}{2}
\]
Therefore:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{2N(N-1)}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]





Choose (N=5) (a (5) grid, 25 sites):


Code
N <- 5
total_pairs <- choose(N^2, 2)

# 1) at least one corner
p_at_least_one_corner <- 1 - choose(N^2 - 4, 2) / total_pairs

# 2) at least one edge
p_at_least_one_edge <- 1 - choose((N - 2)^2, 2) / total_pairs

# 3) adjacent
p_adjacent <- (2 * N * (N - 1)) / total_pairs

p_at_least_one_corner


[1] 0.3


Code
p_at_least_one_edge


[1] 0.88


Code
p_adjacent


[1] 0.1333333






We simulate (B) random samples of 2 distinct sites, and estimate probabilities by proportions (mean(TRUE/FALSE)).
We label each grid cell with a string "i,j" so sampled sites look like coordinates.


Code
set.seed(109)

N <- 5
B <- 10000

# Make a grid whose entries look like "i,j" (row,col)
grid <- matrix("", nrow = N, ncol = N)
for (i in 1:N) {
  for (j in 1:N) {
    grid[i, j] <- paste(i, j, sep = ",")
  }
}

corners <- c(grid[1,1], grid[1,N], grid[N,1], grid[N,N])
edges   <- c(grid[1, ], grid[N, ], grid[2:(N-1), 1], grid[2:(N-1), N])

at_least_one_corner <- logical(B)
at_least_one_edge   <- logical(B)
adjacent            <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  picks <- sample(grid, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  s1 <- picks[1]
  s2 <- picks[2]

  rc1 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s1, ",")[[1]])
  rc2 <- as.numeric(strsplit(s2, ",")[[1]])

  r1 <- rc1[1]; c1 <- rc1[2]
  r2 <- rc2[1]; c2 <- rc2[2]

  at_least_one_corner[b] <- sum(c(s1 %in% corners, s2 %in% corners)) > 0
  at_least_one_edge[b]   <- sum(c(s1 %in% edges,   s2 %in% edges))   > 0

  same_row_adj <- (r1 == r2) * (abs(c1 - c2) == 1)
  same_col_adj <- (c1 == c2) * (abs(r1 - r2) == 1)
  adjacent[b] <- sum(c(same_row_adj, same_col_adj)) > 0
}

title: Lab 2
text:
Before the 2D grid, we practice the same ideas on a 1D line of 5 sites.
We label sites as:
\[
S = \{1,2,3,4,5\}
\]
We choose 2 distinct sites without replacement, so the equally likely sample space is all 2-site sets:
\[
|S_2| = \binom{5}{2} = 10
\]


The “end sites” are 1 and 5.
Use the complement (“no ends” means both picks are from {2,3,4}):
\[
P(\text{at least one end})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{\binom{5}{2}}
= 1 - \frac{3}{10}
= \frac{7}{10}
\]



Adjacent pairs are:

{1,2}, {2,3}, {3,4}, {4,5} (4 pairs total)

So:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{4}{\binom{5}{2}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4
\]





Code
set.seed(109)

sites <- 1:5
B <- 10000

at_least_one_end <- logical(B)
adjacent <- logical(B)

for (b in 1:B) {
  draws <- sample(sites, size = 2, replace = FALSE)
  a <- draws[1]
  c <- draws[2]

  # "at least one end" using the Lab 1 sum idea
  at_least_one_end[b] <- sum(c(a == 1, a == 5, c == 1, c == 5)) > 0

  # adjacent on a line if they differ by 1
  adjacent[b] <- (abs(a - c) == 1)
}

mean(at_least_one_end)


[1] 0.7015


Code
mean(adjacent)


[1] 0.4008

title: Lab 2
text:
We are choosing 2 distinct sites from (N^2) equally likely sites.
There are 4 corners and (N^2 - 4) non-corners.
Use the complement (“no corners”):
\[
P(\text{at least one corner})
= 1 - P(\text{no corners})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{N^2 - 4}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]

title: Lab 2
text:
Edges (including corners) total (4N - 4) sites, so non-edges total:
\[
N^2 - (4N - 4) = (N-2)^2
\]
Use the complement (“no edges”):
\[
P(\text{at least one edge})
= 1 - P(\text{no edges})
= 1 - \frac{\binom{(N-2)^2}{2}}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]

title: Lab 2
text:
We will say two sites are adjacent if they are 4-neighbors (up/down/left/right).


In an (NN) grid:

Horizontal adjacent pairs: (N(N-1))
Vertical adjacent pairs: (N(N-1))

So total adjacent (unordered) pairs:
\[
2N(N-1)
\]
Total possible unordered pairs of sites:
\[
\binom{N^2}{2}
\]
Therefore:
\[
P(\text{adjacent}) = \frac{2N(N-1)}{\binom{N^2}{2}}
\]

title: Lab 7
text:
Quick guide: 95% interval choices + conditions

A) One mean, population effectively infinite (no FPC)
Use a t-interval:
\[
\bar{x} \pm t^{*}_{df=n-1}\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
Conditions (typical intro stat version):
- SRS (or random sample), independent observations
- Data are roughly symmetric (normal) or n is moderately large (greater than 30)



B) One mean, finite population (use FPC)
Use a t-interval with finite population correction (SRS without replacement from a known finite list):
\[
\bar{x} \pm t^{*}_{df=n-1}\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
\]
Conditions:
- SRS without replacement from a finite population of known size N
- Data are roughly symmetric (normal) or n is moderately large (greater than 30)



C) One proportion, population effectively infinite (no FPC), large-sample “normal” CI
\[
\hat{p} \pm z^{*} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
\]
Conditions: - SRS (or random sample), independent - Large counts: \(n\hat{p} \ge 10\) and \(n(1-\hat{p})\ge 10\)



D) One proportion, finite population (use FPC), large-sample “normal” CI
\[
\hat{p} \pm z^{*} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
\]
Conditions: - SRS without replacement from a finite population of known size N - Large counts: \(n\hat{p} \ge 10\) and \(n(1-\hat{p})\ge 10\)



E) One proportion, small sample (or rare event): exact binomial CI
If \(n\hat{p} < 10\) or \(n(1-\hat{p}) < 10\), prefer an exact binomial CI (Clopper–Pearson): - In R: binom.test(x, n) then .$conf.int Conditions: - Fixed n, independent binary trials with common success probability (binomial model) - (If sampling without replacement from a finite population and n/N is small, binomial is a reasonable approximation.)

title: Lab 7
section: Extra (optional): confidence intervals with the survey package
text:
Extra (optional): confidence intervals with the survey package
Pick either the mean subsection or the proportion subsection below—each is self-contained.


One mean (Problems 1–2)
Idea: For a numeric outcome, svymean(~measurement, design) estimates the population mean. confint(...) gives a 95% interval with a t-style SE when the design is a simple random sample (same spirit as t.test).
Steps (minimal):

Build a data.frame with one row per sampled unit and a column for the numeric measurement (e.g. minutes, chair count).
Call svydesign(ids = ~1, data = ...) — ids = ~1 means “no clusters; each row is its own primary unit.”
Finite population (Problem 2): add a column equal to N (total population size) on every row and set fpc = ~N in svydesign. Infinite population (Problem 1): omit fpc.
confint(svymean(~measurement, design)) — interval for the population mean.

This matches Problems 1–2 with less algebra in R.


Code
library(survey)

# --- Problem 1 style (no FPC): same x as above ---
x <- c(12, 18, 25, 9, 31, 16, 20, 22, 14, 3, 19, 11, 24, 30, 15, 15, 21, 26, 13, 28, 10, 23, 29, 18, 33)
d1 <- data.frame(x = x)
w1 <- svydesign(ids = ~1, data = d1)
confint(svymean(~x, w1))   # matches the usual one-sample t interval


     2.5 %   97.5 %
x 16.37208 22.42792


Code
# --- Problem 2 style (FPC): N = 300 classrooms, same x as above ---
N <- 300
x2 <- c(28, 30, 32, 27, 27, 33, 33, 26, 30, 30, 28, 29, 35, 27, 32, 32, 28, 30, 29, 33, 26, 34, 28, 31, 30, 27, 32, 29, 33, 28)
d2 <- data.frame(x = x2, N = N)
w2 <- svydesign(ids = ~1, data = d2, fpc = ~N)
confint(svymean(~x, w2))


     2.5 %   97.5 %
x 29.04327 30.75673





One proportion (Problems 3–4)
Idea: For a Yes/No (or 0/1) outcome, svymean(~outcome, design) gives \(\hat{p}\). confint(...) uses a large-sample normal approximation for the proportion (same spirit as the Wald formulas in the quick guide).
Steps (minimal):

Build a data.frame with one row per sampled unit and a column for the Yes/No (or 0/1) outcome.
Call svydesign(ids = ~1, data = ...) — ids = ~1 means “no clusters; each row is its own primary unit.”
Finite population (Problem 4): add a column equal to N (total population size) on every row and set fpc = ~N in svydesign. Infinite population (Problem 3): omit fpc.
confint(svymean(~outcome, design)) — interval for the population proportion.

This matches Problems 3–4 with less algebra in R.


Code
library(survey)

# --- Problem 3 style (no FPC): n = 220, x = 80 with part-time job ---
job <- c(rep(TRUE, 80), rep(FALSE, 220 - 80))
d3  <- data.frame(job = job)
w3  <- svydesign(ids = ~1, data = d3)
confint(svymean(~job, w3))


             2.5 %    97.5 %
jobFALSE 0.5726529 0.7000743
jobTRUE  0.2999257 0.4273471


Code
# --- Problem 4 style (FPC): N = 1500, n = 108, x = 20 “>50 years” ---
d4 <- data.frame(
  old50 = c(rep(TRUE, 20), rep(FALSE, 108 - 20)),
  N     = 1500
)
w4 <- svydesign(ids = ~1, data = d4, fpc = ~N)
confint(svymean(~old50, w4))


               2.5 %    97.5 %
old50FALSE 0.7439121 0.8857175
old50TRUE  0.1142825 0.2560879

$ fl           <chr> "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p…
$ class        <chr> "compact", "compact", "compact", "compact", "compact", "c…

title: Lab 9
text:
Part 1: One numerical variable
Let’s describe hwy (highway MPG), a numerical variable.

1A) Check type


Code
class(mpg$hwy)


[1] "integer"




1B) Compute mean and SD


Code
mpg %>%
  summarise(
    mean_hwy = mean(hwy),
    sd_hwy = sd(hwy)
  )


# A tibble: 1 × 2
  mean_hwy sd_hwy
     <dbl>  <dbl>
1     23.4   5.95




1C) Make a histogram


Code
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = hwy)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, boundary = 0, color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of highway MPG",
    x = "Highway MPG (hwy)",
    y = "Count"
  )

title: Lab 9
text:
Part 2: One categorical variable
Now describe drv (drive type), a categorical variable.

2A) Check type and categories


Code
class(mpg$drv)


[1] "character"


Code
levels(mpg$drv)


NULL




2B) Compute counts and proportions


Code
mpg %>%
  count(drv, name = "count") %>%
  mutate(proportion = count / sum(count))


# A tibble: 3 × 3
  drv   count proportion
  <chr> <int>      <dbl>
1 4       103      0.440
2 f       106      0.453
3 r        25      0.107

title: Lab 9
section: Part 3: Change variable type (numeric codes -> categories)
text:
Part 3: Change variable type (numeric codes -> categories)
In mpg, cyl (number of cylinders) is numeric, but in many analyses we treat it as categories (4, 5, 6, 8) rather than a continuous scale.


Code
class(mpg$cyl)


[1] "integer"


Convert cyl to a factor:


Code
mpg2 <- mpg %>%
  mutate(cyl = factor(cyl))

class(mpg2$cyl)


[1] "factor"


Code
levels(mpg2$cyl)


[1] "4" "5" "6" "8"


Now compute count and proportion for cyl categories:


Code
mpg2 %>%
  count(cyl, name = "count") %>%
  mutate(proportion = count / sum(count))


# A tibble: 4 × 3
  cyl   count proportion
  <fct> <int>      <dbl>
1 4        81     0.346 
2 5         4     0.0171
3 6        79     0.338 
4 8        70     0.299

title: Lab 9
text:
Part 4: filter() with conditions
Use filter() to keep only rows meeting conditions.
Examples:


Code
# Cars with highway MPG at least 30
mpg %>%
  filter(hwy >= 30) %>%
  select(manufacturer, model, hwy) %>%
  head()


# A tibble: 6 × 3
  manufacturer model    hwy
  <chr>        <chr>  <int>
1 audi         a4        31
2 audi         a4        30
3 chevrolet    malibu    30
4 honda        civic     33
5 honda        civic     32
6 honda        civic     32




Code
# SUVs only
mpg %>%
  filter(class == "suv") %>%
  select(manufacturer, model, class, hwy) %>%
  head()


# A tibble: 6 × 4
  manufacturer model              class   hwy
  <chr>        <chr>              <chr> <int>
1 chevrolet    c1500 suburban 2wd suv      20
2 chevrolet    c1500 suburban 2wd suv      15

4 chevrolet    c1500 suburban 2wd suv      17
5 chevrolet    c1500 suburban 2wd suv      17
6 chevrolet    k1500 tahoe 4wd    suv      19




Code
# SUVs with 4-wheel drive
mpg %>%
  filter(class == "suv", drv == "4") %>%
  select(manufacturer, model, class, drv, hwy) %>%
  head()


# A tibble: 6 × 5
  manufacturer model           class drv     hwy
  <chr>        <chr>           <chr> <chr> <int>
1 chevrolet    k1500 tahoe 4wd suv   4        19

title: Lab 9
text:
Part 5: group_by() + summarise()
Now summarize a numerical variable by groups.

5A) Mean and SD of highway MPG by drive type


Code
mpg %>%
  group_by(drv) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_hwy = mean(hwy),
    sd_hwy = sd(hwy)
  )


# A tibble: 3 × 4
  drv       n mean_hwy sd_hwy
  <chr> <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>
1 4       103     19.2   4.08
2 f       106     28.2   4.21
3 r        25     21     3.66




5B) Mean and SD of highway MPG by vehicle class


Code
mpg %>%
  group_by(class) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_hwy = mean(hwy),
    sd_hwy = sd(hwy)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_hwy))


# A tibble: 7 × 4
  class          n mean_hwy sd_hwy
  <chr>      <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>
1 compact       47     28.3   3.78
2 subcompact    35     28.1   5.38
3 midsize       41     27.3   2.14
4 2seater        5     24.8   1.30
5 minivan       11     22.4   2.06
6 suv           62     18.1   2.98
7 pickup        33     16.9   2.27

title: Lab 9
text:
Practice tasks (student work)
Use mpg to complete the following:

Pick one numerical variable (cty, hwy, or displ) and report mean + SD.
Make a histogram for that variable.
Pick one categorical variable (drv, class, or manufacturer) and report count + proportion.
Use filter() to keep rows with hwy >= 28 and drv == "f". Show first 10 rows of manufacturer, model, hwy, and drv.
Use group_by(class) and report mean cty by class.
Convert one coded variable to factor (for example cyl) and list its levels.

title: Lab 9
text:
Wrap-up
Today you practiced a tidyverse workflow for one-variable description:

identify variable type,
compute the right summary for that type,
visualize numerical variables,
and apply filter() and group_by() for focused comparisons.

These steps will be reused throughout project work and upcoming labs.
